title: Gastroenterology Consultation - Acute Upper GI Hemorrhage
author: Dr. William Thompson

sections.consultReason: Emergent gastroenterology consultation for acute upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability in a 72-year-old male on warfarin anticoagulation.
sections.historyOfPresentIllness:
Thank you for this emergent consultation. I have independently evaluated this patient and reviewed the ED records.

Mr. Morrison is a 72-year-old male with atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 3.8, supratherapeutic), heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, CKD stage 3b, and coronary artery disease who presents with 2 days of melena, one episode of coffee-ground emesis, and a near-syncopal episode at home. He was found to be in hemorrhagic shock on arrival (BP 88/52, HR 112) with hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL (baseline 11.5).

The patient reports vague epigastric discomfort for the past 1-2 weeks. Critically, he has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg TID for 3 days for knee pain, which he obtained over the counter without consulting his physician. He was unaware of the interaction between NSAIDs and warfarin.

Nasogastric lavage in the ED returned coffee-ground material without active bright red blood, suggesting recent but not actively brisk upper GI hemorrhage.

The patient has no prior history of peptic ulcer disease, GI bleeding, Helicobacter pylori infection, liver disease, or esophageal varices. He has been on omeprazole 20 mg daily for GERD. He has no history of H. pylori testing or prior endoscopy.
relevantMedications: Ibuprofen 400 mg TID x 3 days (OTC, DISCONTINUED)
Omeprazole 20 mg daily (switched to IV pantoprazole drip)
sections.physicalExamFindings:
General: Pale, ill-appearing male, resting comfortably after initial fluid resuscitation. More alert than on initial ED presentation.
Vitals post-resuscitation: BP 102/64, HR 98 irregular, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 2L NC.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild epigastric tenderness without guarding or rebound. No hepatosplenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.
Rectal: Melena in vault, guaiac positive. No hemorrhoids or masses.
sections.labReview:
Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL (critical, baseline 11.5)
INR: 3.8 (supratherapeutic, goal 2-3)
BUN: 68, Creatinine: 2.8 (elevated BUN:Cr ratio >20:1, consistent with upper GI source + prerenal AKI)
Lactate: 3.4 (improving from initial presentation)
Albumin: 3.1 (mildly low, nutritional/chronic disease)
LFTs: Within normal limits — no evidence of underlying liver disease
Platelet count: 198 (adequate for hemostasis)
sections.assessment:
Acute upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage, most likely secondary to peptic ulcer disease.

The clinical presentation is highly consistent with a gastric or duodenal ulcer given the following risk factor profile:

1. NSAID use (ibuprofen) — the single most important modifiable risk factor for peptic ulcer disease. NSAIDs inhibit COX-1-mediated prostaglandin synthesis in the gastric mucosa, impairing the mucosal defense barrier.

2. Concurrent anticoagulation with warfarin — does not cause ulcers per se, but dramatically increases the risk of bleeding from pre-existing mucosal lesions. The supratherapeutic INR (3.8) further amplifies this risk.

3. Concurrent aspirin use — additive risk with NSAIDs for gastric mucosal injury.

4. The NSAID-warfarin interaction is particularly dangerous: ibuprofen inhibits platelet aggregation (COX-1 inhibition), displaces warfarin from plasma protein binding sites (increasing free warfarin concentration), and directly injures gastric mucosa. This "triple threat" of anticoagulation potentiation, platelet inhibition, and mucosal injury makes GI hemorrhage both more likely and more severe.

Differential diagnosis includes:
- Peptic ulcer disease (most likely) — gastric or duodenal
- Erosive gastritis/gastropathy (NSAID-induced)
- Dieulafoy lesion
- Gastric or esophageal malignancy (less likely given acute onset)
- Cameron lesions (hiatal hernia erosions)
- Angiodysplasia (less likely in upper GI tract)

The elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20:1) is consistent with an upper GI source, as digested blood in the GI tract increases urea absorption.
plan: 1. URGENT ESOPHAGOGASTRODUODENOSCOPY (EGD) — planned for tomorrow morning (9/13/2023) at first available endoscopy suite time. The patient must be hemodynamically stabilized prior to proceeding. I anticipate the EGD will be both diagnostic and therapeutic.

2. Pre-EGD optimization: Continue IV pantoprazole infusion (80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr). Correct coagulopathy — target INR <1.5 before EGD (4F-PCC and Vitamin K already administered by ED team). Transfuse to hemoglobin >7 (ideally >8 given CAD).
3. NPO after midnight. Continue NGT to monitor for active hemorrhage (if bright red blood returns, will proceed with emergent EGD).
4. Erythromycin 250 mg IV will be administered 30-60 minutes prior to EGD as a prokinetic agent to improve gastric visualization by promoting gastric emptying.

5. During EGD: Full examination of esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. Therapeutic intervention as indicated — prepared for epinephrine injection, bipolar cautery, hemoclipping, and/or argon plasma coagulation depending on findings.

6. Will obtain CLO (Campylobacter-like organism) test during EGD for Helicobacter pylori — this is essential as H. pylori is an independent risk factor for peptic ulcer disease and eradication dramatically reduces ulcer recurrence.
7. Post-EGD: Will determine need for continued IV PPI vs transition to oral PPI based on endoscopic findings and stigmata of recent hemorrhage (Forrest classification).

8. Long-term considerations: This patient will need indefinite proton pump inhibitor therapy. The decision regarding anticoagulation (warfarin continuation, dose modification, or alternative agent) will need to involve Cardiology given his atrial fibrillation and stroke risk. This is a complex risk-benefit discussion that should occur after the acute event is managed.
9. Patient and wife counseled regarding the planned procedure, including risks of sedation, perforation, bleeding, and need for possible intervention. Informed consent will be obtained pre-procedure.
sections.riskStratification:
Glasgow-Blatchford Score: 15 (high risk — age >70, hemoglobin <10, systolic BP <90, heart rate >100, BUN >25, melena present, syncope present). This score confirms the need for urgent inpatient endoscopic evaluation and intervention.

Rockall Score (pre-endoscopy): 5 (age >70, shock present, comorbidities present). High risk for rebleeding and mortality.
sections.followUp: I will perform the EGD personally tomorrow morning. Will round on the patient pre-procedure. Available by pager for any acute change in clinical status overnight, including hemodynamic instability, large-volume hematemesis, or drop in hemoglobin requiring re-evaluation of the need for emergent endoscopy.

attestation:
I have independently evaluated this patient, reviewed all available records and diagnostic data, and formulated the above assessment and plan. I have discussed my recommendations with the primary team (Dr. Brown, Hospital Medicine) and the MICU team (Dr. Mitchell, Critical Care).

Electronically signed by: William Thompson, MD, FACG
Date/Time: 9/12/2023 7:30 AM
